title: CECS 460 -- CPU Architectures and Core Comparisons
learning_objectives: Distinguish between ISA and microarchitecture, and explain why CPU labels alone do not determine performance, power, or timing behavior
Explain RISC and CISC as design philosophies and evaluate when each can make sense in a real system
Compare Harvard, von Neumann, and modified Harvard memory organizations in terms of instruction/data paths, bandwidth, contention, and programmer visibility
Compare single-core, symmetric multicore, and heterogeneous SoC designs in terms of predictability, concurrency, power, synchronization, and debugging complexity
Interpret big.LITTLE and mixed-criticality systems as architectural responses to competing performance, power, timing, and safety goals
Use approximate performance and power numbers as engineering intuition without treating them as universal constants
Measure and explain how heavy background work, shared state, or poor task isolation on a multicore ESP32 can increase latency, jitter, missed events, or unstable timing

hardware.wiring: Breadboarded ESP32 timing lab. Required external hardware: 1 LED, 1 current-limiting resistor, 1 pushbutton, breadboard, and jumper wires. The lab demonstrates timing instability caused by heavy background work and shared-buffer contention in a multicore ESP32 program.

reference_code.label: Chapter 3 -- ESP32 Core Timing and Shared-Buffer Lab Firmware
reference_code.description: ESP32 classroom firmware for a Chapter 3 bug-fix lab. Students inspect timing telemetry and revise the starter code so the timing-critical LED/event path is less affected by heavy background work and shared-buffer contention.

reference_code.label: Chapter 3 -- Reference Solution Firmware
reference_code.description: Reference firmware showing cleaner task separation, reduced shared-state interference, and improved timing behavior for the Chapter 3 multicore lab.

steps.title: 3.1 -- ISA, Microarchitecture, and Instruction Set Philosophy
steps.subtitle: Why CPU labels alone do not tell the whole story

content_blocks.text: A processor's ISA is the interface visible to software: instructions, registers, encodings, memory behavior, and exceptions. The microarchitecture is the hardware design that runs that ISA: pipeline, cache organization, branch prediction, issue width, clock rate, and execution units.

Two processors can run the same ISA and still behave very differently. The ISA explains software compatibility; the microarchitecture explains much of the real performance, power, and timing behavior.

content_blocks.heading: RISC vs. CISC as engineering tradeoffs
rows: Instruction style
Smaller, more regular instruction set; often load/store
Larger, more varied instruction set; often richer addressing modes
Typical strengths
Predictability, power efficiency, simpler pipelines
Backward compatibility, mature software ecosystem, code density
Common examples
ARM Cortex-M, RISC-V, Xtensa
Typical embedded use
Microcontrollers, IoT, real-time systems
Gateways, industrial PCs, systems with legacy desktop software

content_blocks.text: Avoid the slogan RISC = faster. In embedded systems, RISC often wins because it is simpler, lower power, and more predictable. High-performance CISC processors can still be much faster in absolute wall-clock time.

question.text: Which statement best separates ISA from microarchitecture?
choices: The ISA is the chip's clock speed, while the microarchitecture is the source code.
The ISA is what software targets, while the microarchitecture is how a specific processor implements it.
The ISA only matters for CISC processors, while microarchitecture only matters for RISC processors.
The ISA determines battery size, while microarchitecture determines the programming language.
question.explanation: The ISA defines the software-visible interface. The microarchitecture is the actual hardware organization that implements that interface.

steps.title: 3.2 -- Memory Architecture
steps.subtitle: Harvard, von Neumann, modified Harvard, and contention

content_blocks.text: Harvard and von Neumann describe how instruction and data access are organized. Harvard designs separate instruction and data paths, while von Neumann designs use a shared path and a simpler unified model.

content_blocks.heading: Harvard vs. von Neumann
rows: Instruction and data paths
Separated
Shared
Bandwidth behavior
Higher potential throughput
More contention between instruction fetch and data access
Programmer view
May be separate or partly unified
Usually one unified address space
Embedded relevance
Useful for deterministic or high-throughput loops
Simple conceptual model for software

content_blocks.text: Harvard architecture does not always mean two completely separate physical memory chips. Many embedded SoCs use a modified Harvard approach: separate instruction/data paths near the core, but a mostly unified address space for C code.

Caches improve average performance but can add variable latency. Scratchpad or tightly coupled memory is smaller and more manual, but it can make timing easier to predict for interrupt handlers or time-critical code.

question.text: What is the main practical advantage of a Harvard-style organization?
choices: It always uses two physical memory chips.
Instruction fetch and data access can use separate paths, reducing contention.
It eliminates all cache misses.
It only works with x86 processors.
question.explanation: The key idea is separate instruction and data paths. The physical memory organization can vary across real systems.

steps.title: 3.3 -- Single-Core, Multicore, and Heterogeneous SoCs
steps.subtitle: Choosing the core arrangement that fits the workload

content_blocks.text: Single-core systems are often simpler, easier to debug, and easier to analyze for worst-case timing. Multicore systems can improve responsiveness by separating work, but they add synchronization, shared-resource contention, and debugging complexity.

content_blocks.heading: Core organization tradeoffs
rows: Single-core
Simple, predictable, easier worst-case timing analysis
Less concurrency and less task isolation
Symmetric multicore
Better responsiveness and parallel task separation
Synchronization overhead, race conditions, and shared-resource contention
Heterogeneous / big.LITTLE
Can match different workloads to different cores
More complex scheduling, power management, and system design

content_blocks.text: In a timing-sensitive embedded system, multicore is useful only when the work is separated carefully. A heavy background task can still interfere with a timing-critical task through shared memory, locks, queues, cache effects, or poor scheduling.
Mixed criticality means tasks with different timing, safety, or reliability requirements share the same system. One task may be safety-critical while another is only a user-interface or logging task.

question.text: Which is the main tradeoff of multicore systems compared with single-core systems?
choices: They eliminate all timing problems.
They improve concurrency but add synchronization and debug complexity.
They can only be used with CISC processors.
They remove the need for shared memory.
question.explanation: Multicore can help separate work, but shared data and coordination create new complexity.

steps.title: 3.4 -- Processor Families as Design Choices
steps.subtitle: Do not memorize the catalog; match the core to the constraint

content_blocks.text: Processor families are best understood as design choices. The useful question is not 'which core is best?' but 'which core best fits this product's timing, power, cost, compatibility, and software requirements?'

content_blocks.heading: Example architecture choices
rows: Tiny battery sensor
Low-power MCU such as MSP430, Cortex-M0+, or small RV32
Predictability, sleep modes, low cost, low energy
Wi-Fi/BLE IoT node
ESP32-class Xtensa or RISC-V SoC
Integrated radios, crypto, peripherals, and embedded software support
Hard real-time control
Cortex-R or high-end Cortex-M with TCM
Deterministic timing and fast interrupt response
Industrial gateway with legacy software
x86/x86-64 platform
Compatibility with existing OS and application ecosystem
Smartphone-like rich UI
Cortex-A-class heterogeneous SoC
High performance, multimedia, GPUs/NPUs, and power management

question.text: Why might an engineer choose an x86 or x86-64 processor in an embedded product?
choices: Because x86 always has the lowest power consumption.
Because legacy software, Windows/Linux support, or a mature software ecosystem may matter more than minimal power.
Because x86 is required for Harvard architecture.
Because x86 eliminates synchronization bugs.
question.explanation: x86 is often chosen when compatibility and software ecosystem outweigh embedded-style power or determinism concerns.

content_blocks.text: Architecture tradeoffs are easier to understand when you have a rough sense of scale. The numbers below are order-of-magnitude examples, not values to memorize. They vary with processor design, workload, compiler, memory system, cache state, voltage/frequency, and power-management mode.

content_blocks.heading: Order-of-magnitude examples
rows: Embedded RISC CPI
Often around 1-2 CPI for simple code on efficient microcontroller-class cores
Short pipelines and simple instruction flows can make timing easier to reason about
High-performance CPU CPI
Can be below 1 CPI for highly parallel instruction streams or much higher when stalled
Wide CPUs can do a lot per cycle, but stalls and memory delays can dominate
Power per MHz
Small MCUs may be in the microwatts-per-MHz range; desktop/server-class CPUs are far higher
This helps explain coin-cell MCUs versus processors that need much more power and cooling
Lock or synchronization cost
Can range from tens to hundreds or more cycles, especially under contention
Multicore speedup is not free when tasks share data
Extra-core leakage / idle cost
An additional core can still consume power even when lightly used
More cores can improve responsiveness but may hurt battery life if poorly managed
Energy savings from parallelism or big.LITTLE
Can be tens of percent for suitable workloads
Savings depend on whether work can be split efficiently and assigned to the right core

content_blocks.text: Use these values for scale. The lab's main point is not an exact cycle count; it is that heavy work, shared state, and contention can create measurable timing problems.

question.text: How should students use approximate CPI, power, and synchronization numbers from this chapter?
choices: As exact constants that apply to every processor.
As rough engineering intuition that depends on implementation and workload.
As proof that multicore always saves energy.
As values that only apply to CISC processors.
question.explanation: The numbers are useful for scale, but real values depend on the processor, workload, compiler, memory behavior, and power mode.

steps.title: Lab -- Timing Stability and Shared-Buffer Behavior on the ESP32
steps.subtitle: Observe, fix, and explain a multicore timing problem

content_blocks.text: In this lab, the ESP32 runs a producer/consumer timing test using a small shared buffer. The starter firmware includes intentionally inefficient timing-path behavior, including heavy consumer work and shared-state access that can contribute to missed events or unstable timing.
Required hardware: ESP32 DevKit, LED, current-limiting resistor, pushbutton, breadboard, and jumper wires. The LED indicates timing activity, and the button starts the test run.
If the result still shows poor timing, inspect the producer/consumer behavior, shared buffer access, task delays/yields, and which work belongs on the timing-critical path.

question.text: Your ESP32 will automatically submit timing telemetry for this lab.
question.explanation: The firmware submits a timing result string such as [timing:produced=X consumed=Y missed=Z backlog=B producer_core=A consumer_core=B status=PASS].

steps.title: Lab Reflection -- Explain the Timing Behavior
steps.subtitle: Use your measured result to connect code behavior to architecture

content_blocks.text: The point of the lab is not just to make the output pass. It is to connect the measured behavior to Chapter 3 ideas: concurrency, contention, shared state, isolation, and the cost of coordinating work on a multicore system.

content_blocks.heading: Ideas to reference in your explanation:
items: A timing-critical task competing with heavy background work
Shared-buffer contention or inefficient shared-state access
Latency, jitter, missed events, or backlog
Why isolating or reducing heavy work on the timing path can improve responsiveness
Why multicore can help but also adds synchronization and debugging complexity

question.text: Explain why heavy background work or inefficient shared-buffer access can increase timing jitter, latency, missed events, or backlog in a multicore embedded system. Then explain how cleaner task separation or reducing work on the timing-critical path improves behavior.
question.placeholder: Example: The timing-critical path was affected because ...
question.hint: Use terms like contention, shared buffer, latency, jitter, missed events, backlog, core placement, scheduling, or isolation if they apply.

questions.text: A wearable health monitor must sample a sensor at a predictable interval, run from a small battery, and remain easy to debug. Would you choose a single-core, multicore, or heterogeneous design first? Justify your answer using timing predictability, power, and software complexity.

A product must run legacy x86 software and a desktop-class operating system, even though it will consume more power than a small MCU. Explain why a designer might choose a CISC/x86 platform anyway, and name the tradeoff.

Compare Harvard and von Neumann memory architectures. Explain how separate instruction/data paths can reduce contention, and why modified Harvard systems do not always require two completely separate physical memories.

A multicore ESP32 program has a timing-critical producer/LED path and a heavy consumer/background path using shared state. Explain why the device may show jitter, missed events, latency, or backlog, and how cleaner task separation or reduced shared-buffer work can help.

The chapter gives approximate CPI, power-per-MHz, synchronization, leakage, and energy-saving examples. Explain how students should use these numbers and why they should not treat them as universal constants.

Which statement best separates ISA from microarchitecture?

A. The ISA is what software targets; microarchitecture is how a specific processor implements it.
B. The ISA is the chip's clock speed; microarchitecture is the programming language.
C. ISA only matters for RISC; microarchitecture only matters for CISC.
D. ISA and microarchitecture always mean the same thing.

Which statement best captures the revised chapter's RISC/CISC takeaway?

A. RISC is always faster than CISC.
B. CISC is obsolete in all embedded systems.
C. RISC often helps with simplicity, power, and predictability, while CISC/x86 can be valuable for compatibility and mature software ecosystems.
D. RISC processors cannot use caches.

Which statement best describes Harvard architecture?

A. Instructions and data always share one bus.
B. Instructions and data use separate paths.
C. Harvard architecture only exists in desktop CPUs.
D. Harvard architecture eliminates the need for RAM.

Which is the main tradeoff of multicore systems compared with single-core systems?

A. They eliminate all timing problems.
B. They reduce synchronization complexity.
C. They improve concurrency but add synchronization and debug complexity.
D. They can only be used in x86 systems.

big.LITTLE refers to:

A. A heterogeneous architecture with performance and efficiency cores.
B. A symmetric multicore system with equal cores only.
C. A memory hierarchy with large and small cache lines.
D. A programming model for Harvard architectures.
True or False: Harvard architecture always means two completely separate physical memories.
Your ESP32 will automatically submit timing telemetry for this lab. Do not type anything here; the firmware will publish the result string automatically.
Reflection: Explain why heavy background work, shared-buffer contention, or poor task isolation can harm timing stability in a multicore embedded system.
